      Prime brokerage
   =20
 =20

What does the trading business of a big bank do? One crude model might be: =
In the olden days, perhaps 20 years ago, the trading division=C2=A0did trad=
ing. It did customer facilitation=C2=A0trading (dealing, market making): bu=
ying=C2=A0securities that customers wanted to sell, selling=C2=A0securities=
 that customers wanted to buy, using the bank=E2=80=99s balance sheet and c=
ollecting a spread for that service. And it did=C2=A0proprietary trading: b=
uying securities that=C2=A0the bank=C2=A0wanted to buy (because it thought =
they would go up) and selling securities that it wanted to sell. There is s=
ome conceptual distinction between those businesses, but they are related; =
in either case, the bank=E2=80=99s traders used its balance sheet to buy an=
d sell securities and took the risk that those securities might go down.=C2=
=A0
Since the 2008 financial crisis, that has become somewhat less true. In the=
 US, proprietary trading, as a business for big banks, has been more or les=
s outlawed for a decade by the Volcker Rule. Customer facilitation trading =
is=C2=A0still a big business, but it is   widely believed that banks have s=
caled back their market making for a variety of regulatory and risk-appetit=
e reasons: The Volcker Rule complicates market making, increased capital re=
quirements have made it less economical for banks to use their balance shee=
ts to trade securities, and the generally more boring and conservative cult=
ure=C2=A0of post-crisis banks has left them less interested in taking big t=
rading risks. And so more of what the=C2=A0trading desks do now is matching=
 up buyers and sellers=C2=A0without=C2=A0using the bank=E2=80=99s own balan=
ce sheet to buy and sell stuff.
You might expect that someone would step in to fill that gap: If banks aren=
=E2=80=99t=C2=A0as much=C2=A0in the business of using their balance sheets=
=C2=A0to provide liquidity, and if they are no longer=C2=A0at all=C2=A0in t=
he business of making directional bets on stocks and bonds and derivatives,=
 then somebody else should be doing those things.
Somebody is, and to a first approximation it is hedge funds. I mean, it=E2=
=80=99s other people too =E2=80=94=C2=A0maybe bond exchange-traded funds ar=
e the new liquidity providers for bonds, maybe high-frequency electronic tr=
ading firms are the new liquidity providers for stocks, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0but it=E2=80=99s often hedge funds. Hedge funds are the obvious, direct =
replacement for banks=E2=80=99 proprietary trading desks: Banks used to be =
in the business of making big levered directional bets on stocks and bonds;=
 now they aren=E2=80=99t. Hedge funds still are, the skills are fairly tran=
sferable, and in fact a lot of prop traders did=C2=A0leave banks for hedge =
funds after the Volcker Rule. But even in market making, as banks step back=
 from providing liquidity with their balance sheets, hedge funds have done =
more of it. We  talked last month about new US rules that will regulate som=
e hedge funds as securities =E2=80=9Cdealers,=E2=80=9D because they are in =
the business of   taking the other sides of fundamental investors=E2=80=99 =
trades, like banks used to.
When banks did this business in the glory days, it was very levered: Banks =
would buy $100 or $200 or $300 of assets with $10 of their shareholders=E2=
=80=99 capital=C2=A0and a lot of borrowed money. When hedge funds do this b=
usiness =E2=80=A6=C2=A0shouldn=E2=80=99t it also be pretty levered? Maybe n=
ot 30 to 1 like the banks sometimes were in 2006 =E2=80=94=C2=A0that had pr=
oblems
  [1]
=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0but probably this is a business that can be done with =
some borrowed money. Hedge funds should use some of their investors=E2=80=
=99 money, and a lot of borrowed money, to do their trades.
Where do they borrow the money from? Banks! The simple, crude, overstated m=
odel is: In 2004, a bank=E2=80=99s trading division was in the business of=
=C2=A0trading with the bank=E2=80=99s money. In 2024, a bank=E2=80=99s trad=
ing division is increasingly in the business of=C2=A0lending the bank=E2=80=
=99s money to hedge funds, which do the trading.
Again, this is hugely exaggerated, but I find it useful. At IFR, Christophe=
r Whittall=C2=A0reports:

Prime brokerage accounted for more than half banks=E2=80=99 equities revenu=
es in 2023, a record share that underlines how providing financing and mark=
et access to hedge funds is simultaneously anchoring and redefining banks=
=E2=80=99 trading divisions.
The top 30 banks made an unprecedented US$32bn in prime services and future=
s in 2023, according to Vali Analytics, up from US$26bn in 2020 when activi=
ties represented 43% of equity trading revenues. That fast-growing pie, fue=
lled by the breakneck expansion of multi-strategy hedge funds, is encouragi=
ng banks to compete ever more fiercely in a business that only three years =
ago triggered a death spiral at Credit Suisse. ...
Many believe the appeal of financing has only increased over time, arguing =
these activities bring much-needed stability to banks' otherwise volatile t=
rading divisions.
"Financing is attractive because of the size and consistency of the industr=
y wallet. It=E2=80=99s increasingly becoming the means [by] which the large=
st [alternative asset] managers allocate their fee-paying wallet to provide=
rs," said Mike Webb, global head of liquid financing at Barclays. "The bigg=
er reason is that the income stream from financing is stable, repeatable, c=
lient-centric and creates an incredibly accretive return profile."

It is striking how normal=C2=A0this is. People sometimes argue that it is a=
berrant that banks ever got into the business of trading stocks and bonds t=
o begin with. (For a while, it was more or less illegal in the US.) Broadly=
 speaking, in the economy, there are all sorts of businesses that=C2=A0do s=
tuff, and banks are in the meta-business of=C2=A0lending them money to do t=
he stuff. That meta-business is supposed to be=C2=A0safer=C2=A0than the und=
erlying businesses:=C2=A0Instead of=C2=A0being in=C2=A0risky businesses the=
mselves, banks=C2=A0have a diversified collection of senior claims on risky=
 businesses; the other businesses run the risks, and the banks get the rela=
tively safe first claim on their revenues. In some loose sense maybe this i=
s true of securities trading too, and in the long run the right business fo=
r bank trading divisions to be in is=C2=A0lending money to other=C2=A0busin=
esses that do the actual trading.

      Private markets are the new public markets
   =20
 =20

Well, here=E2=80=99s another model for a bank=E2=80=99s trading business. M=
aybe the job of a trading business is to have a lot of clients, to know the=
ir positions well, and to match up their desires. You know that Alice has 1=
00 shares of XYZ stock and wants to sell it, you know that Bob has some cas=
h and is looking to buy 100 shares of XYZ stock, you match them up,=C2=A0ma=
ke the trade happen and collect a commission.
In regular-way trading of US public stocks, this is an almost absurd way to=
 think of the bank=E2=80=99s role: Alice just sends an electronic sell orde=
r, Bob sends a buy order, they cross on some electronic trading venue and n=
o human at the bank needs to have any interaction at all with Alice or Bob.=
 Relatedly, the commissions that the bank can collect on that trade are pre=
tty low; for most retail brokerage platforms they are zero. But banks do so=
metimes put together larger block trades of stock for their customers, wher=
e their market knowledge is more valuable and they can get paid more. And o=
f course a lot of other products =E2=80=94=C2=A0many high-yield bonds,=C2=
=A0asset-backed securities, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0do tend to trade mostly thr=
ough the memory and market knowledge of bank traders. If there are only 100=
 of some bond, and I want to buy them, I don=E2=80=99t know who owns them, =
but maybe some big bank=E2=80=99s trading desk does. And I have to pay that=
 bank to find them for me.
Again, though, regular US listed stocks do not trade like that; they trade =
on anonymous electronic platforms, so banks don=E2=80=99t really have much =
advantage in putting together trades by knowing their clients=E2=80=99 posi=
tions and desires and matching them up.
Private stocks,   on the other hand:

Morgan Stanley=E2=80=99s wealth management arm is giving its clients a chan=
ce to buy and sell coveted shares of private companies before they are avai=
lable to the wider public, as startups weighing initial public offerings in=
creasingly remain private for longer.
The bank=E2=80=99s Private Markets Transaction Desk will assist Morgan Stan=
ley Wealth Management clients seeking to invest in the highly-fragmented an=
d opaque market for private shares, according to a statement Monday. Shares=
 in more than 1,000 so-called unicorns =E2=80=94 private companies valued a=
t over $1 billion =E2=80=94 aren=E2=80=99t available to the general public,=
 the statement showed.

Here is=C2=A0the statement, which adds:
Clients who use the desk can benefit from direct support for one-off sales =
of private company shares. Conversely, clients intent on investing in priva=
te companies can gain access to investments not widely accessible. This off=
ering complements the existing private investment platforms for clients off=
ered through Morgan Stanley Wealth Management.
Yes, right, if you are a Morgan Stanley wealth client and you own shares in=
 a private company and want to sell them, Morgan Stanley would like to know=
 about that. If you are a Morgan Stanley wealth client and you=C2=A0want=C2=
=A0some shares in a private company, Morgan Stanley would like to know abou=
t that too. If it has both sides of the trade, it will put them together, m=
ake its clients happy=C2=A0and make some money. Good service, good fee oppo=
rtunity, and also a business where it is an advantage to have a large clien=
t base. Though: =E2=80=9CIn addition to existing client relationships, it h=
as established relationships with leading secondary marketplaces which can =
help clients gain exposure to a broader market with a focus on transaction =
execution quality.=E2=80=9D Private markets are the new public markets, and=
 this business will eventually be anonymized and electronified too.

   =20
      Elon vs. the lawyers
   =20
 =20

So the obvious thing to say is that  this is absurd:

Lawyers for a Tesla=C2=A0shareholder who helped scuttle Elon Musk=E2=80=99s=
 $55.8 billion compensation package asked a Delaware court on Friday to app=
rove legal fees worth about $5.6 billion.
It would be a record-breaking payday for the attorneys if approved. The att=
orneys asked to be paid almost entirely in Tesla stock, a rare request from=
 lawyers on the winning side of a verdict.
Chancellor Kathaleen McCormick=E2=80=99s January decision to strike down Mu=
sk=E2=80=99s pay package at Tesla was the largest verdict in the history of=
 the Delaware Chancery Court. =C2=A0McCormick ordered Musk, Tesla=E2=80=99s=
 chief executive, to return the stock options he received under his 2018 co=
mpensation package, which the company had valued at a maximum of $55.8 bill=
ion. =E2=80=A6
In Friday=E2=80=99s filing to the court, the shareholder=E2=80=99s attorney=
s asked for more than 29 million shares in Tesla and an additional $1 milli=
on to cover expenses. =E2=80=A6 =E2=80=9CWe are prepared to =E2=80=98eat ou=
r cooking,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D the plaintiff=E2=80=99s lead attorney Greg Var=
allo wrote in Friday=E2=80=99s filing.
=E2=80=9CThe size of the requested award is great because the value of the =
benefit to Tesla that plaintiff=E2=80=99s counsel achieved was massive.=E2=
=80=9D

Tesla Inc.=E2=80=99s shareholders   voted to pay Elon Musk $56 billion=C2=
=A0(in Tesla stock) if he could raise Tesla=E2=80=99s market cap by $600 bi=
llion, and he did, and they were mostly happy, and then some lawyers sued a=
nd got a Delaware court to rescind that compensation package, and now those=
 lawyers=C2=A0want $5.6 billion (in Tesla stock) for taking away Musk=E2=80=
=99s paycheck. I am not by any means an uncritical Elon Musk fan, but I thi=
nk it is obvious to the casual observer that he has created more than 10 ti=
mes as much value, for Tesla=E2=80=99s shareholders, as Greg Varallo has. A=
nd I think it is weird that Delaware law might not=C2=A0allow Tesla=E2=80=
=99s shareholders to vote to pay Elon Musk $56=C2=A0billion, but might=C2=
=A0require=C2=A0them to pay Greg Varallo $5.6 billion without=C2=A0a vote. =
Not very shareholder-friendly!
Still I think it is more fun to consider this as a trade. Here is the trade=
:
=09In 2018, Tesla and Musk struck a deal in which (1) he would work to make=
 Tesla a $650 billion company and (2) if he succeeded, he=E2=80=99d get pai=
d $56 billion.
  [2]

=09Motivated by this deal,
  [3]
=C2=A0Musk worked very hard, slept in the office, sacrificed his personal l=
ife, and succeeded in making Tesla worth as much as $1.2 trillion by 2021. =
(And still more than $600 billion today.)=09Tesla said =E2=80=9Cthanks very=
 much, job well done, pleasure doing business with you,=E2=80=9D put $56 bi=
llion of stock options in a bag, and was about to hand the bag=C2=A0over to=
 Musk.=09These shareholder lawyers said =E2=80=9CStop! Actually we have fou=
nd a way to=C2=A0keep the benefit of that deal=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0the $600=
-plus billion dollars of value that Musk created =E2=80=94=C2=A0without pay=
ing him. There is some fine print in the contract with him that allows Tesl=
a to yoink back those stock options. Let=E2=80=99s do that.=E2=80=9D=09Tesl=
a,=C2=A0as an abstract profit maximizing corporation =E2=80=94=C2=A0not an =
extension of Elon Musk=E2=80=99s will, but the impersonal result of the app=
lication of Delaware corporate law and fiduciary duties =E2=80=94=C2=A0said=
 =E2=80=9Cwell I mean if we can keep the benefit without paying $56 billion=
 for it, that=E2=80=99s better for shareholder value maximization, so let=
=E2=80=99s do it.=E2=80=9D
  [4]



Right? Tesla was going to pay Musk $56 billion for his=C2=A0past=C2=A0work =
creating $600 billion of value. If it can just take back that $56 billion,=
=C2=A0for free, on a technicality, and=C2=A0keep the $600 billion of value,=
 that=E2=80=99s a good trade, it should do it, and maybe it should even giv=
e the lawyers a 10% commission for finding the trade.
  [5]

Now, there is an obvious hole in this analysis.
  [6]
=C2=A0That $56 billion wasn=E2=80=99t just=C2=A0to pay Musk for his past ef=
forts. This is a repeat game. Musk is still Tesla=E2=80=99s chief executive=
 officer, he still makes lots of decisions, he still seems to be important =
to Tesla=E2=80=99s success, and the board and shareholders still want him a=
round. Yoinking back his pay package for the last five years is going to de=
-motivate him for the=C2=A0future.=C2=A0If your model of Musk is that his e=
fforts can create or destroy hundreds of billions of dollars of value for T=
esla =E2=80=94=C2=A0and that he has a lot of good options for how to spend =
his time =E2=80=94=C2=A0then stiffing him on back pay is not a good long-te=
rm decision.
That said, though, Elon Musk himself has made it hard to make that objectio=
n.=C2=A0Before=C2=A0the decision in this case came down, back when Musk sti=
ll theoretically had those $56 billion of stock options, he was   going aro=
und saying in public that he was no longer motivated to do a good job for T=
esla, that he was going to take his best ideas elsewhere, and that he=E2=80=
=99d need tens of billions of dollars in=C2=A0additional=C2=A0stock options=
 to re-motivate him.=C2=A0His=C2=A0position was basically that the $56 bill=
ion was past pay for past performance, and that if Tesla wants his future e=
fforts it needs to give him another huge pay package.=C2=A0And if that=E2=
=80=99s right =E2=80=94=C2=A0if that $56 billion=C2=A0only=C2=A0purchased M=
usk=E2=80=99s last five years of effort, with no future loyalty =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0then, sure, maybe it=E2=80=99s a good deal to take it back.

   =20
      Zombie VCs
   =20
 =20

I used to be an investment banker, and I often went to client meetings, bec=
ause that=E2=80=99s what I was getting paid to do. Had they not paid me to =
do it, I would not have gone to client meetings. I recognize that, in this =
and other ways, I was a=C2=A0bad=C2=A0investment banker. The people who rea=
lly get ahead in investment banking, and in financial services generally, a=
re the ones who would go to client meetings for free, who can=E2=80=99t thi=
nk of a better use of time than meeting with clients, who get=C2=A0social a=
nd emotional and intellectual satisfaction from=C2=A0doing client meetings.=
 I   wrote once about Andrea Orcel, who had to be forcibly prevented from f=
lying to Davos to meet clients=C2=A0when he was between jobs. That is the l=
evel of commitment to client meetings=C2=A0that you want in your financial =
professional.
Anyway  this made me laugh:=C2=A0

When founders are raising money for their startups, the first thing they sh=
ould do is verify the firm they are talking to is actually still in busines=
s, multiple founders and VCs told Business Insider.
"Ask how many checks they have written in the past year and whether they ar=
e actively deploying capital," advised Anna Barber, a partner at M13.
It may seem redundant to ask a venture firm whose only mandate is to invest=
 if they are indeed investing, but VCs say it has become increasingly neces=
sary as firms put on airs to project the image of business as usual even as=
 they are running on fumes.
"Founders end up wasting a lot of time because they'll go research the firm=
s and meet with them and the firms don't even tell them they're not activel=
y investing," said Roy Bahat, head of Bloomberg Beta. "They pretend to go t=
hrough the motions, and then they never make a deal."

Can you imagine=C2=A0pretending=C2=A0to be a venture capitalist? =E2=80=9CW=
ell, I don=E2=80=99t have any money to invest in any startups, but I do lov=
e getting pitched on startups, so what the heck let=E2=80=99s just do some =
meetings anyway.=E2=80=9D Obviously it=E2=80=99s not=C2=A0purely=C2=A0a hob=
by: If you are a venture capitalist who has invested your entire fund and c=
an=E2=80=99t raise a new one, acting like you still have money to invest ma=
kes you seem like a viable VC, which might help you raise a new fund eventu=
ally.

There is also a strong reticence for VCs to admit they are no longer invest=
ing because it immediately brandishes them as a lame duck and makes it almo=
st impossible to raise money or find a new job, according to Bahat.
"It's poison because the second a firm tells that to one person, all of the=
 sudden nobody wants to talk to them anymore," Bahat said. "There is a stro=
ng incentive to basically lie for an extended period of time, or at least p=
retend that you still have money."

But it is=C2=A0a little bit of a hobby:

Telling people you are a VC also gets you into a lot of glitzy parties and =
conferences. No one wants to give that up, says [venture capitalist Brian] =
Brackeen.
"In VC, you don't stop having your job until you say you do, right?," he sa=
id.

Heavens no, you wouldn=E2=80=99t want to give up going to conferences.

  [1] One problem was =E2=80=9Cthen 2008 happened.=E2=80=9D Another problem=
 is that the 30-to-1 leverage came in part through a =E2=80=9Ctoo-big-to-fa=
il subsidy,=E2=80=9D in which bank leverage was implicitly backed by the US=
 government, and post-crisis regulation tried to move away from that.


  [2] This number isn=E2=80=99t right; it=E2=80=99s the number everyone use=
s, and that Tesla disclosed, but it=E2=80=99s an artifact of stock options =
accounting. In actual fact Tesla=E2=80=99s market cap hit $1.2 trillion in =
2021, making the 2018 stock options award worth more than $100 billion to M=
usk. But whatever, $56 billion is good enough.


  [3] One could object to this description, and in fact the thrust of Chanc=
ellor McCormick=E2=80=99s decision is that Musk didn=E2=80=99t actually nee=
d this pay package to motivate him, because, among other things, he was alr=
eady a huge Tesla shareholder and stood to (and did) increase his fortune b=
y tens of billions of dollars if Tesla succeeded. This strikes me as probab=
ly mostly correct, but on the other hand both Musk and Tesla=E2=80=99s boar=
d *said* that this pay package was necessary to motivate him, so who knows.


  [4] Obviously it=E2=80=99s worse for *Musk*, Tesla=E2=80=99s single bigge=
st shareholder, but that=E2=80=99s not how abstract profit maximizing corpo=
rations think: The job is to make the corporation better off (on behalf of =
all shareholders), not to make particular (or even all!) shareholders bette=
r off personally. Or that is the classic view anyway.


  [5] Though, obviously, it=E2=80=99s even better for Tesla if it can *not*=
 pay *them* either. That too is in the hands of the judge. Probably judges =
are more keen on paying lawyers than they are on paying Elon Musk. Still, y=
ou can bet there are other lawyers, representing other shareholders, who ar=
e working on objections of the form =E2=80=9CWell, judge, you have already =
clawed back the $56 billion from Musk, so what do you need to pay these law=
yers for? Let the shareholders keep that money too.=E2=80=9D


  [6] Well, several. Another important hole is that my analysis pretends th=
at there are two entities, Musk and Tesla, negotiating at arm's length. Of =
course the other point of Chancellor McCormick=E2=80=99s decision is that t=
hat wasn=E2=80=99t true, and that Musk controlled Tesla and was essentially=
 negotiating with himself. Again that strikes me as mostly correct.

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

5px;">Prime brokerage</h2>
</td>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What does the trading business of a big bank d=
o? One crude model might be: In the olden days, perhaps 20 years ago, the t=
rading division=C2=A0did trading. It did <em>customer facilitation=C2=A0tra=
ding</em> (dealing, market making): buying=C2=A0securities that customers w=
anted to sell, selling=C2=A0securities that customers wanted to buy, using =
the bank=E2=80=99s balance sheet and collecting a spread for that service. =
And it did=C2=A0<em>proprietary trading: </em>buying securities that=C2=A0<=
em>the bank=C2=A0</em>wanted to buy (because it thought they would go up) a=
nd selling securities that it wanted to sell. There is some conceptual dist=
inction between those businesses, but they are related; in either case, the=
 bank=E2=80=99s traders used its balance sheet to buy and sell securities a=
nd took the risk that those securities might go down.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Since the 2008 financial crisis, that has beco=
me somewhat less true. In the US, proprietary trading, as a business for bi=
g banks, has been more or less outlawed for a decade by <a href=3D"https://=
link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZmVkZ=
XJhbHJlc2VydmUuZ292L3N1cGVydmlzaW9ucmVnL3ZvbGNrZXItcnVsZS5odG0/607f07ceef4b=
8524a319a23cB6884be9d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targe=
t=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: s=
olid; background-color: #ccc;">the Volcker Rule</a>. Customer facilitation =
trading is=C2=A0still a big business, but it is <a href=3D"https://link.mai=
l.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnL=
mNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTUtMDYtMDMvcGVvcGxlLWFyZS13b3JyaWVkLWFib3V0=
LWJvbmQtbWFya2V0LWxpcXVpZGl0eT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwMzA0MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWV=
kaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDAzMDQmdXRtX2NhbX=
BhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB3e2bb6cd" itemprop=3D"StoryL=
ink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; tex=
t-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-colo=
r: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  widely b=
elieved</a> that banks have scaled back their market making for a variety o=
f regulatory and risk-appetite reasons: The Volcker Rule complicates market=
 making, increased capital requirements have made it less economical for ba=
nks to use their balance sheets to trade securities, and the generally more=
 boring and conservative culture=C2=A0of post-crisis banks has left them le=
ss interested in taking big trading risks. And so more of what the=C2=A0tra=
ding desks do now is matching up buyers and sellers=C2=A0<em>without=C2=A0<=
/em>using the bank=E2=80=99s own balance sheet to buy and sell stuff.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">You might expect that someone would step in to=
 fill that gap: If banks aren=E2=80=99t=C2=A0<em>as much=C2=A0</em>in the b=
usiness of using their balance sheets=C2=A0to provide liquidity, and if the=
y are no longer=C2=A0<em>at all=C2=A0</em>in the business of making directi=
onal bets on stocks and bonds and derivatives, then somebody else should be=
 doing those things.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Somebody is, and to a first approximation it i=
s hedge funds. I mean, it=E2=80=99s other people too =E2=80=94=C2=A0maybe b=
ond exchange-traded funds are the new liquidity providers for bonds, maybe =
high-frequency electronic trading firms are the new liquidity providers for=
 stocks, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0but it=E2=80=99s often hedge funds. Hedge fund=
s are the <em>obvious</em>, direct replacement for banks=E2=80=99 proprieta=
ry trading desks: Banks used to be in the business of making big levered di=
rectional bets on stocks and bonds; now they aren=E2=80=99t. Hedge funds st=
ill are, the skills are fairly transferable, and in fact a lot of prop trad=
ers did=C2=A0<a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34561=
117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud2FsbHN0cmVldGFuZHRlY2guY29tL2NhcmVlcnMvZXgtZ29s=
ZG1hbi1zYWNocy1wcm9wLXRyYWRlcnMtZmFpbC1hdC1oZWRnZS1mdW5kcy9kL2QtaWQvMTI2Nzc=
4MGQ0MWQuaHRtbD8/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd0aec969" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" =
itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-dec=
oration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0=
00000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">leave banks for=
 hedge funds</a> after the Volcker Rule. But even in market making, as bank=
s step back from providing liquidity with their balance sheets, hedge funds=
 have done more of it. We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.co=
m/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGl=
jbGVzLzIwMjQtMDItMDcvaGVkZ2UtZnVuZHMtYXJlLWRlYWxlcnMtbm93P2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMD=
QyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0b=
V90ZXJtPTI0MDMwNCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB=
87eeb5dd" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" =
style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wi=
dth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgro=
und-color: #ccc;"> talked last month</a> about new US rules that will regul=
ate some hedge funds as securities =E2=80=9Cdealers,=E2=80=9D because they =
are in the business of <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/c=
lick/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbG=
VzLzIwMjMtMDktMTQvcGVvcGxlLWFyZS13b3JyaWVkLWFib3V0LXRoZS1iYXNpcy10cmFkZT9jb=
XBpZD1CQkQwMzA0MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3=
c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDAzMDQmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cB1c2aeaab" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targ=
et=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bo=
rder-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: =
solid; background-color: #ccc;">  taking the other sides</a> of fundamental=
 investors=E2=80=99 trades, like banks used to.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When banks did this business in the glory days=
, it was very levered: Banks would buy $100 or $200 or $300 of assets with =
$10 of their shareholders=E2=80=99 capital=C2=A0and a lot of borrowed money=
. When hedge funds do this business =E2=80=A6=C2=A0shouldn=E2=80=99t it als=
o be pretty levered? Maybe not 30 to 1 like the banks sometimes were in 200=
6 =E2=80=94=C2=A0that had problems<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#foot=
note-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-b=
ottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !=
important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;=
">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0but probably this is a business that can be done w=
ith some borrowed money. Hedge funds should use some of their investors=E2=
=80=99 money, and a lot of borrowed money, to do their trades.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Where do they borrow the money from? Banks! Th=
e simple, crude, overstated model is: In 2004, a bank=E2=80=99s trading div=
ision was in the business of=C2=A0<em>trading with the bank=E2=80=99s money=
</em>. In 2024, a bank=E2=80=99s trading division is increasingly in the bu=
siness of=C2=A0<em>lending the bank=E2=80=99s money to hedge funds, which d=
o the trading</em>.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Again, this is hugely exaggerated, but I find =
it useful. At IFR, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click=
/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuaWZyZS5jb20vc3RvcnkvNDQzMzU4Ni9wcmltZS1icm=
9rZXJhZ2UtdGhlLW11bHRpLWJpbGxpb24tZG9sbGFyLWNhc2gtY293LXJlZGVmaW5pbmctYmFua=
3MtdHJhZGluZy1kaXZpc2lvbnMtdDByZncxYnIwNQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBdf2e11bd=
" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D=
"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px=
; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-colo=
r: #ccc;">Christopher Whittall=C2=A0reports</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Prime brokerage accounted for more than half b=
anks=E2=80=99 equities revenues in 2023, a record share that underlines how=
 providing financing and market access to hedge funds is simultaneously anc=
horing and redefining banks=E2=80=99 trading divisions.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The top 30 banks made an unprecedented US$32bn=
 in prime services and futures in 2023, according to Vali Analytics, up fro=
m US$26bn in 2020 when activities represented 43% of equity trading revenue=
s. That fast-growing pie, fuelled by the breakneck expansion of multi-strat=
egy hedge funds, is encouraging banks to compete ever more fiercely in a bu=
siness that only three years ago triggered a death spiral at Credit Suisse.=
 ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Many believe the appeal of financing has only =
increased over time, arguing these activities bring much-needed stability t=
o banks' otherwise volatile trading divisions.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"Financing is attractive because of the size a=
nd consistency of the industry wallet. It=E2=80=99s increasingly becoming t=
he means [by] which the largest [alternative asset] managers allocate their=
 fee-paying wallet to providers," said Mike Webb, global head of liquid fin=
ancing at Barclays. "The bigger reason is that the income stream from finan=
cing is stable, repeatable, client-centric and creates an incredibly accret=
ive return profile."</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is striking how <em>normal=C2=A0</em>this i=
s. People sometimes argue that it is aberrant that banks ever got into the =
business of trading stocks and bonds to begin with. (For a while, it was <a=
 href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34561117.257373/aHR0=
cHM6Ly93d3cuZmVkZXJhbHJlc2VydmVoaXN0b3J5Lm9yZy9lc3NheXMvZ2xhc3Mtc3RlYWdhbGw=
tYWN0/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB9535a812" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">more or less illegal in =
the US</a>.) Broadly speaking, in the economy, there are all sorts of busin=
esses that=C2=A0<em>do stuff</em>, and banks are in the meta-business of=C2=
=A0<em>lending them money to do the stuff</em>. That meta-business is suppo=
sed to be=C2=A0<em>safer=C2=A0</em>than the underlying businesses:=C2=A0Ins=
tead of=C2=A0being in<em>=C2=A0</em>risky businesses themselves, banks=C2=
=A0have a diversified collection of senior claims on risky businesses; the =
other businesses run the risks, and the banks get the relatively safe first=
 claim on their revenues. In some loose sense maybe this is true of securit=
ies trading too, and in the long run the right business for bank trading di=
visions to be in is=C2=A0lending money to other=C2=A0businesses that do the=
 actual trading.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=

5px;">Private markets are the new public markets</h2>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Well, here=E2=80=99s another model for a bank=
=E2=80=99s trading business. Maybe the job of a trading business is to have=
 a lot of clients, to know their positions well, and to match up their desi=
res. You know that Alice has 100 shares of XYZ stock and wants to sell it, =
you know that Bob has some cash and is looking to buy 100 shares of XYZ sto=
ck, you match them up,=C2=A0make the trade happen and collect a commission.=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In regular-way trading of US public stocks, th=
is is an almost absurd way to think of the bank=E2=80=99s role: Alice just =
sends an electronic sell order, Bob sends a buy order, they cross on some e=
lectronic trading venue and no human at the bank needs to have any interact=
ion at all with Alice or Bob. Relatedly, the commissions that the bank can =
collect on that trade are pretty low; for most retail brokerage platforms t=
hey are zero. But banks do sometimes put together larger block trades of st=
ock for their customers, where their market knowledge is more valuable and =
they can get paid more. And of course a lot of other products =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0many high-yield bonds,=C2=A0asset-backed securities, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0do tend to trade mostly through the memory and market knowledge of bank =
traders. If there are only 100 of some bond, and I want to buy them, I don=
=E2=80=99t know who owns them, but maybe some big bank=E2=80=99s trading de=
sk does. And I have to pay that bank to find them for me.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Again, though, regular US listed stocks do not=
 trade like that; they trade on anonymous electronic platforms, so banks do=
n=E2=80=99t really have much advantage in putting together trades by knowin=
g their clients=E2=80=99 positions and desires and matching them up.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yes, right, if you are a Morgan Stanley wealth=

to existing client relationships, it has established relationships with lea=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">So the obvious thing to say is that <a href=3D=
"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93=

nk" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-botto=
m-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; bac=
kground-color: #ccc;"> this is absurd</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Lawyers for a Tesla=C2=A0shareholder who helpe=
d scuttle Elon Musk=E2=80=99s $55.8 billion compensation package asked a De=
laware court on Friday to approve legal fees worth about $5.6 billion.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It would be a record-breaking payday for the a=
ttorneys if approved. The attorneys asked to be paid almost entirely in Tes=
la stock, a rare request from lawyers on the winning side of a verdict.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Chancellor Kathaleen McCormick=E2=80=99s Janua=
ry decision to strike down Musk=E2=80=99s pay package at Tesla was the larg=
est verdict in the history of the Delaware Chancery Court. =C2=A0McCormick =
ordered Musk, Tesla=E2=80=99s chief executive, to return the stock options =
he received under his 2018 compensation package, which the company had valu=
ed at a maximum of $55.8 billion. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In Friday=E2=80=99s filing to the court, the s=
hareholder=E2=80=99s attorneys asked for more than 29 million shares in Tes=
la and an additional $1 million to cover expenses. =E2=80=A6 =E2=80=9CWe ar=
e prepared to =E2=80=98eat our cooking,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D the plaintiff=E2=
=80=99s lead attorney Greg Varallo wrote in Friday=E2=80=99s filing.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThe size of the requested award is gr=
eat because the value of the benefit to Tesla that plaintiff=E2=80=99s coun=
sel achieved was massive.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Tesla Inc.=E2=80=99s shareholders <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMzEvZWxvbi1tdXNrLWlzL=
W92ZXJwYWlkP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMDQyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRt=
X3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDMwNCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHV=
mZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB87671459" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"=
itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none=
 !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border=
-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  voted to pay</a> Elon Musk=
 $56 billion=C2=A0(in Tesla stock) if he could raise Tesla=E2=80=99s market=
 cap by $600 billion, and he did, and they were mostly happy, and then some=
 lawyers sued and got a Delaware court to rescind that compensation package=
, and now those lawyers=C2=A0want $5.6 billion (in Tesla stock) for taking =
away Musk=E2=80=99s paycheck. I am not by any means an uncritical Elon Musk=
 fan, but I think it is obvious to the casual observer that he has created =
more than 10 times as much value, for Tesla=E2=80=99s shareholders, as Greg=
 Varallo has. And I think it is weird that Delaware law might not<em>=C2=A0=
allow</em> Tesla=E2=80=99s shareholders to vote to pay Elon Musk $56=C2=A0b=
illion, but might=C2=A0<em>require=C2=A0</em>them to pay Greg Varallo $5.6 =
billion <em>without=C2=A0</em>a vote. Not very shareholder-friendly!</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still I think it is more fun to consider this =
as a trade. Here is the trade:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">In 2018, Tesla and Musk struck a deal=
 in which (1) he would work to make Tesla a $650 billion company and (2) if=
 he succeeded, he=E2=80=99d get paid $56 billion.<a class=3D"footnote-index=
" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display=
: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>
</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Motivated by this deal,<a class=
=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !=
important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Musk worked very hard, slept in the office, sacrificed his person=
al life, and succeeded in making Tesla worth as much as $1.2 trillion by 20=
21. (And still more than $600 billion today.)</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bo=
ttom: 5px;">Tesla said =E2=80=9Cthanks very much, job well done, pleasure d=
oing business with you,=E2=80=9D put $56 billion of stock options in a bag,=
 and was about to hand the bag=C2=A0over to Musk.</li>=09<li style=3D"margi=
n-bottom: 5px;">These shareholder lawyers said =E2=80=9CStop! Actually we h=
ave found a way to=C2=A0<em>keep the benefit of that deal=C2=A0</em>=E2=80=
=94=C2=A0the $600-plus billion dollars of value that Musk created =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0<em>without paying him</em>. There is some fine print in the contract=
 with him that allows Tesla to yoink back those stock options. Let=E2=80=99=
s do that.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Tesla,=C2=A0<e=
m>as an abstract profit maximizing corporation </em>=E2=80=94=C2=A0not an e=
xtension of Elon Musk=E2=80=99s will, but the impersonal result of the appl=
ication of Delaware corporate law and fiduciary duties =E2=80=94=C2=A0said =
=E2=80=9Cwell I mean if we can keep the benefit without paying $56 billion =
for it, that=E2=80=99s better for shareholder value maximization, so let=E2=
=80=99s do it.=E2=80=9D<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" sty=
le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; =
background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>
</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Right? Tesla was going to pay Musk $56 billion=
 for his=C2=A0<em>past=C2=A0</em>work creating $600 billion of value. If it=
 can just take back that $56 billion,=C2=A0<em>for free</em>, on a technica=
lity, and=C2=A0<em>keep the $600 billion of value</em>, that=E2=80=99s a go=
od trade, it should do it, and maybe it should even give the lawyers a 10% =
commission for finding the trade.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footn=
ote-5" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !i=
mportant; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;"=
>
<span>[5]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Now, there is an obvious hole in this analysis=
.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-6" style=3D"color: #000000; =
text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-c=
olor: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: tran=
sparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[6]</span>
</a>=C2=A0That $56 billion wasn=E2=80=99t <em>just=C2=A0</em>to pay Musk fo=
r his past efforts. This is a repeat game. Musk is still Tesla=E2=80=99s ch=
ief executive officer, he still makes lots of decisions, he still seems to =
be important to Tesla=E2=80=99s success, and the board and shareholders sti=
ll want him around. Yoinking back his pay package for the last five years i=
s going to de-motivate him for the=C2=A0<em>future.</em>=C2=A0If your model=
 of Musk is that his efforts can create or destroy hundreds of billions of =
dollars of value for Tesla =E2=80=94=C2=A0and that he has a lot of good opt=
ions for how to spend his time =E2=80=94=C2=A0then stiffing him on back pay=
 is not a good long-term decision.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That said, though, Elon Musk himself has made =
it hard to make that objection.=C2=A0<em>Before=C2=A0</em>the decision in t=
his case came down, back when Musk still theoretically had those $56 billio=
n of stock options, he was <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.c=
om/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydG=
ljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDEtMTYvbW9yZ2FuLXN0YW5sZXktbGVha2VkLXNvbWUtYmxvY2stdHJhZGVzP=
2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMDQyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1u=
ZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDMwNCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ce=
ef4b8524a319a23cBb891a648" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" t=
arget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important;=
 border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-styl=
e: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  going around saying in public</a> that=
 he was no longer motivated to do a good job for Tesla, that he was going t=
o take his best ideas elsewhere, and that he=E2=80=99d need tens of billion=
s of dollars in=C2=A0<em>additional=C2=A0</em>stock options to re-motivate =
him.=C2=A0<em>His=C2=A0</em>position was basically that the $56 billion was=
 past pay for past performance, and that if Tesla wants his future efforts =
it needs to give him another huge pay package.=C2=A0And if that=E2=80=99s r=
ight =E2=80=94=C2=A0if that $56 billion=C2=A0<em>only=C2=A0</em>purchased M=
usk=E2=80=99s last five years of effort, with no future loyalty =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0then, sure, maybe it=E2=80=99s a good deal to take it back.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I used to be an investment banker, and I often=
 went to client meetings, because that=E2=80=99s what I was getting paid to=
 do. Had they not paid me to do it, I would not have gone to client meeting=
s. I recognize that, in this and other ways, I was a=C2=A0<em>bad=C2=A0</em=
>investment banker. The people who really get ahead in investment banking, =
and in financial services generally, are the ones who would go to client me=
etings for free, who can=E2=80=99t think of a better use of time than meeti=
ng with clients, who get=C2=A0social and emotional and intellectual satisfa=
ction from=C2=A0doing client meetings. I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloom=
bergbusiness.com/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9v=
cGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMTktMDItMTEveW91LWNhbi1zb2NpYWxpemUtd2hpbGUtZ2FyZGV=
uaW5nP2NtcGlkPUJCRDAzMDQyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdX=
JjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDMwNCZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/60=
7f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBc094e750" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsc=
ope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impo=
rtant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-botto=
m-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  wrote once</a> about Andrea Orce=
l, who had to be forcibly prevented from flying to Davos to meet clients=C2=
=A0when he was between jobs. That is the level of commitment to client meet=
ings=C2=A0that you want in your financial professional.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/34561117.257373/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYnVzaW5lc3NpbnNpZGVyLmNvb=
S8yMDI0LXllYXItb2YtdmMtcmVja29uaW5nLTIwMjQtMg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB71ae=

e=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width:=
 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-=
color: #ccc;"> this made me laugh</a>:=C2=A0</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When founders are raising money for their star=
tups, the first thing they should do is verify the firm they are talking to=
 is actually still in business, multiple founders and VCs told Business Ins=
ider.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"Ask how many checks they have written in the =
past year and whether they are actively deploying capital," advised Anna Ba=
rber, a partner at M13.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It may seem redundant to ask a venture firm wh=
ose only mandate is to invest if they are indeed investing, but VCs say it =
has become increasingly necessary as firms put on airs to project the image=
 of business as usual even as they are running on fumes.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"Founders end up wasting a lot of time because=
 they'll go research the firms and meet with them and the firms don't even =
tell them they're not actively investing," said Roy Bahat, head of Bloomber=
g Beta. "They pretend to go through the motions, and then they never make a=
 deal."</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Can you imagine=C2=A0<em>pretending=C2=A0</em>=
to be a venture capitalist? =E2=80=9CWell, I don=E2=80=99t have any money t=
o invest in any startups, but I do love getting pitched on startups, so wha=
t the heck let=E2=80=99s just do some meetings anyway.=E2=80=9D Obviously i=
t=E2=80=99s not=C2=A0<em>purely=C2=A0</em>a hobby: If you are a venture cap=
italist who has invested your entire fund and can=E2=80=99t raise a new one=
, acting like you still have money to invest makes you seem like a viable V=
C, which might help you raise a new fund eventually.</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is also a strong reticence for VCs to ad=
mit they are no longer investing because it immediately brandishes them as =
a lame duck and makes it almost impossible to raise money or find a new job=
, according to Bahat.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"It's poison because the second a firm tells t=
hat to one person, all of the sudden nobody wants to talk to them anymore,"=
 Bahat said. "There is a strong incentive to basically lie for an extended =
period of time, or at least pretend that you still have money."</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But it is=C2=A0a little bit of a hobby:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Telling people you are a VC also gets you into=
 a lot of glitzy parties and conferences. No one wants to give that up, say=
s [venture capitalist Brian] Brackeen.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"In VC, you don't stop having your job until y=
ou say you do, right?," he said.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Heavens no, you wouldn=E2=80=99t want to give =
up going to conferences.</p>
